paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 17: The Last Runner (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Sec 1 boy who is asked at the last minute to run the fourth leg of the inter-class relay, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [10 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format, pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 6 short-answer items (Q1–Q6) and a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q7) are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
My name is Aaron. I am in Sec 1E. I have never been good at sports. In Primary 6, I always finished near the back during the 2.4km run. In Sec 1, I joined the Library Club, not any sport.

Paragraph 2
On Wednesday afternoon, our PE teacher Mr Lee called me to the staffroom. He told me that two boys in my class were down with fever and one had gone on an overseas school trip. The inter-class relay was on Friday. He needed a fourth runner. He looked at me kindly and asked if I would try.

Paragraph 3
I wanted to say no. I knew I would lose the race for 1E. But Mr Lee did not push me. He simply waited. In the end I nodded, because I did not know how to refuse him politely.

Paragraph 4
On Thursday evening, I could not sleep. I lay in bed in our HDB flat in Yishun and stared at the ceiling fan. I thought about my classmates' faces if we came last. At three in the morning I was still awake, listening to the MRT trains starting up.

Paragraph 5
On Friday at the school field, my hands were cold even though the sun was already hot. The first three runners from 1E did their best. When the baton came to me, we were in fourth place. I gripped the baton so tightly that my fingers hurt.

Paragraph 6
And then I ran. I do not remember thinking about anything. I only remember the sound of my own breathing, very loud in my ears, and the white line on the track moving past my feet. Halfway down the home straight, I overtook the boy from 1F. Near the finish line, I passed the boy from 1D as well. We came third out of seven classes.

Paragraph 7
At the end of the race, I bent over with my hands on my knees, trying to breathe. Mr Lee walked over. He did not say 'well done' or 'good job'. He said one sentence: 'Effort is also a result.' Then he patted me on the shoulder and walked away.

Paragraph 8
On the bus home that afternoon, I kept thinking about what Mr Lee had said. I had heard the same sentence many times before, on posters and in assemblies. But for the first time, I understood what it actually meant. I had not won. I had not even come second. But I had done something that I, last Wednesday, would have said was impossible. That, too, was a result.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice, simplified band; not from a past examination paper. HDB: Singapore public housing flats; PE: Physical Education; inter-class relay: a Singapore school event.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what evidence is given that Aaron has never been good at sports? [1]
questions.answer: in Primary 6 he always finished near the back during the 2.4km run, and in Sec 1 he joined the Library Club instead of any sport

questions.stem: Q2. In Paragraph 2, why did Mr Lee need a fourth runner for the inter-class relay? [1]
questions.answer: two boys in Aaron's class were down with fever and one had gone on an overseas school trip, so there were not enough runners for the team

questions.stem: Q3. What does the phrase 'I gripped the baton so tightly that my fingers hurt' (Paragraph 5) suggest about Aaron at that moment? [1]
questions.answer: he was extremely nervous and tense — his tight grip shows how much pressure he was feeling as he was about to run his leg of the race

questions.stem: Q4. In Paragraph 6, what is the effect of the detail that Aaron only remembers the sound of his own breathing and the white line moving past his feet? [1]
questions.answer: it shows how completely focused he was during the race — he was so absorbed in running that he was not aware of anything else, which is unusual for someone who has never been a runner

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 7, why did Mr Lee not say 'well done' or 'good job' but instead say 'Effort is also a result'? [1]
questions.answer: he wanted to make Aaron understand that the value of what he did was not in winning, but in trying his best — a generic congratulation would not have taught him this lesson

questions.stem: Q6. What does the ending 'But I had done something that I, last Wednesday, would have said was impossible. That, too, was a result' (Paragraph 8) reveal about Aaron's view of himself? [1]
questions.answer: he now sees that he is capable of more than he had believed — his sense of what is possible has expanded, and he has learned that trying hard at something difficult is itself a real achievement, not just winning

sections.instruction: Q7. Aaron's dominant feeling shifts as the days leading up to and during the relay pass. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: reluctant / proud / focused / suspicious / relieved / annoyed / encouraged / sleepy. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Last Runner' narrative in Exercise 1 above. Aaron's feelings shift over the course of the story — from being reluctant when Mr Lee asks him to run the fourth leg of the relay, to being deeply focused during the race itself, to a wave of relief when he realises that 1E has come third out of seven, ending in being encouraged on the bus home when he finally understands what Mr Lee meant by 'effort is also a result'. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q7(i). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3, as he stands in the staffroom and finally nods at Mr Lee because he does not know how to refuse him politely? [1]
Q7(ii). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6, as he runs down the track hearing only his own breathing and watching the white line move past his feet? [1]
Q7(iii). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling at the very end of Paragraph 6, when he realises that 1E has come third out of seven classes? [1]
Q7(iv). What best describes Aaron's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8, on the bus home as he understands for the first time what Mr Lee meant by 'effort is also a result'? [1]
